text.title: rate-limiting Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for rate-limiting. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use sliding window (ZSET-based) or GCRA (redis-cell) for user-facing endpoints. Use fixed window only for low-stakes counters (e.g., internal admin polling). Never default to fixed window without explicit justification.
rule.rationale: Fixed window allows 2x the limit in a burst at the window boundary (end of old + start of new window). Sliding window distributes evenly. For public APIs, the burst behavior is exploitable.

rule.statement: All rate limiters in production must be backed by Redis or Valkey. In-memory (dict-based) limiters have independent state per replica and provide no protection once there is more than one process.
rule.rationale: A fleet of N app replicas with in-memory limiters allows N times the configured limit. Redis provides shared atomic state across all replicas.

rule.statement: In the sliding window Redis ZSET implementation, compare the count AFTER the zadd, not before. The count must include the current request. Use a Lua script to make the check-and-add atomic.
rule.rationale: A race condition exists if count is checked before zadd: two concurrent requests both see count less than limit, both add, both succeed despite exceeding the limit. Atomicity via Lua eliminates the race.

rule.statement: Always clamp refilled tokens to min(bucket_size, current + elapsed * rate). Without the clamp, long idle periods accumulate unlimited tokens and allow unbounded bursts.
rule.rationale: A user idle for 1 hour against a 100 req/s bucket would accumulate 360,000 tokens without the clamp. The min() limits burst to the designed maximum regardless of idle time.

rule.statement: Always emit X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, and Retry-After (on 429) on every rate-limited response. Also support the IETF draft RateLimit-* headers for modern clients.
rule.rationale: Standard headers allow clients to implement backoff without parsing error bodies. Missing Retry-After causes clients to retry immediately, generating more 429s and increasing load.

rule.statement: Compose multiple keys per request: ip + user + apikey + tenant. Apply the limit on any one key being exceeded. This prevents a single compromised API key from bypassing IP limits and vice versa.
rule.rationale: Single-key limiters have blind spots. A CGNAT IP behind many users penalizes all of them. A leaked API key with IP rotation bypasses IP limits. Composing keys closes both gaps.

rule.statement: Apply X-Forwarded-For parsing with a trusted-proxy allowlist when the app is behind a CDN. Never use request.client.host directly — it will be the CDN's IP, limiting all traffic as one entity.
rule.rationale: Behind a CDN, request.client.host is always the CDN edge IP. All users share one rate limit counter, causing all traffic to be throttled simultaneously when any user hits the limit.
